ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for a spicy beef taco energy bowl. FOODON_00002473 (beef dish) is the closest defensible ancestor class. A more specific term for assembled salad bowls with seasoned beef is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (beef meat dish) is the closest defensible match. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for a taco-seasoned beef energy bowl assembled with salad vegetables.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite assembled bowl dish. SNOMED CT coverage for mixed restaurant-style bowl dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g (1 bowl), as listed on the GrabFood menu, providing approximately 450 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, ground, 85% lean / 15% fat, cooked, pan-browned; combined with USDA entries for black beans, corn, salad vegetables, and cheese to estimate composite bowl nutrition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for a spicy beef taco energy bowl. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries for: ground beef (85% lean, cooked), black beans (boiled), sweet corn (cooked), mixed salad greens, tomatoes, capsicum, cheddar cheese, and salsa. The input record's per_100g values (energy 128.57 kcal, protein 8.57 g, fat 7.14 g, carbohydrate 8.57 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained with cross-validation against USDA component data. Sodium at 257.14 mg/100g is plausible given taco seasoning contribution.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Analogous estimate based on black bean and mixed vegetable bowl with seasoned meat
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a spicy beef taco energy bowl. GI estimated at approximately 45 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors: black beans (GI ~30, University of Sydney GI Database) and corn (GI ~52). The weighted average of these components, moderated by high protein (8.57 g/100g) and dietary fibre (1.71 g/100g) from the legume and vegetable base, supports a composite GI estimate of ~45. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 30.0) / 100 = 13.5; adjusted to 10.8 accounting for fibre offset (available carbohydrate = 8.57 - 1.71 = 6.86 g/100g × 350 g / 100 = 24.0 g available carb per serving; GL = 45 × 24.0 / 100 = 10.8).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 257 mg/100g (900 mg per 350 g serving) from taco seasoning, salsa, and cheese
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced seasoning and omit added salsa and tortilla chips, as a full 350 g serving delivers approximately 900 mg sodium — nearly 40% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (8.57 g/100g) with low GI (~45) supported by black bean fibre and protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low glycaemic index (~45) and high protein content (8.57 g/100g) support moderate glycaemic control; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or omit tortilla chips and monitor corn portions to avoid refined carbohydrate spikes, keeping total carbohydrate intake within their individualised meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (2.86 g/100g), trans fat (0.14 g/100g), and sodium (257 mg/100g) from seasoned ground beef and cheese
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat from ground beef and cheese (2.86 g/100g) and trans fat (0.14 g/100g) elevates LDL cholesterol risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean beef or substitute with grilled chicken, request low-sodium seasoning, and limit cheese to reduce overall saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory red meat (arachidonic acid) and nightshade vegetables (capsicum, tomatoes)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Red meat is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute spicy beef with grilled chicken or legumes and monitor tolerance to nightshade vegetables (capsicum, tomatoes) which may exacerbate joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Energy Bowl with Spicy Beef Taco (À La Carte) is a Western-style assembled salad bowl sold as an à la carte option on GrabFood in Singapore. It features pan-cooked spicy seasoned ground beef (taco-spiced) served over a base of mixed salad greens, black beans, sweet corn, diced tomatoes, and capsicum, topped with shredded cheddar cheese and salsa. The dish is inspired by Tex-Mex taco bowl cuisine and adapted for the Singapore market. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 128.6 kcal, 8.6 g protein, 7.1 g fat (2.9 g saturated), 8.6 g carbohydrate (1.4 g sugar), 1.7 g dietary fibre, and 257 mg sodium. A standard serving is 350 g (approximately 450 kcal). The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low) due to the dominant black bean and vegetable carbohydrate base moderated by high protein and fibre. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and high protein, but high concern for hypertension and rheumatoid arthritis due to sodium from taco seasoning and pro-inflammatory red meat respectively. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal certification without verification. It contains potential allergens including dairy (cheese) and gluten (taco seasoning, tortilla chips).

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries for the component ingredients (ground beef, black beans, corn, salad vegetables, cheese, salsa). No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. The input record's per_100g values are consistent with the USDA composite estimate and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for black beans and corn. Confidence is medium rather than low because the macronutrient profile is internally consistent and cross-validated against USDA component data.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this composite assembled taco bowl are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as of 2024. Values would require a full laboratory analysis of the assembled dish. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.14 g/100g from the input record, consistent with small amounts of trans fat present in commercially cooked ground beef. No zero-macro justification required as protein_g (8.57), fat_g (7.14), and carbohydrate_g (8.57) are all non-zero. GI estimated at ~45 (low) using analogous black bean bowl data from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Halal status unverified — beef sourcing and taco seasoning ingredients require certification confirmation. The input source listed 'USDA FoodData Central — Western Dish Nutritional Reference' has been retained as the nutrient evidence basis with the adaptation noted above.
